request.system: You are a regulatory analyst. Write a 2-3 sentence storyline summary (≤500 chars total) of the regulatory pressure history for this prediction-market contract. For 'retrospective' kind, write past tense and reference the actual resolution. For 'active' kind, write present tense and note what's still pending. Return JSON {text: ...}.
request.user:
Contract: Will the US enter a recession in 2026?
Kind: active
Timeline (20 events):
- 2026-05-04 [A] Economic Policy Statements to TBAC: 2026 - 2nd Quarter​ — Treasury policy statements citing BEA/BLS data or recession risk can modestly shift the probability that NBER will date a 2026 peak.
- 2026-04-30 [background] 2026-04-30 - New Jersey Employment Report: March 2026 — NJ March 2026 employment is a state-level labor report that offers only weak, indirect signal about a national NBER 2026 peak; minor effect on odds.
- 2026-04-23 [A] Apr 23, 2026 - Final Rule on Revisions to the Community Bank Leverage Ratio (CBLR) Framework | FIL-19-2026 — FDIC CBLR rule revisions affect community bank capital and lending, slightly altering near-term recession risk but do not directly determine NBER dating.
- 2026-04-23 [background] Community Bank Leverage Ratio: Final Rule — An OCC final rule on community bank leverage can slightly affect credit supply and macro risks (thus recession odds) but is indirect and not determinative of an NBER 2026 peak.
- 2026-04-23 [background] Agencies Finalize Changes to Community Bank Leverage Ratio — Changes to community bank leverage rules can modestly affect lending and growth, slightly shifting recession odds but do not directly indicate an NBER 2026 peak.
- 2026-04-02 [background] Consolidated Reports of Condition and Income for First Quarter 2026 — Q1 2026 bank condition reports provide data on financial stress that can modestly shift recession odds, but do not deterministically indicate an NBER 2026 peak.
- 2026-03-31 [background] Stephen I Miran: Prospects for shrinking the Federal Reserve Bank's balance sheet — BIS analysis of Fed balance-sheet shrinkage implies tighter policy and higher near-term recession risk, slightly raising chance of an NBER 2026 peak but not determinative.
- 2026-03-19 [background] Regulatory Capital Rule: Category I and II Banking Organizations, Banking Organizations with Significant Trading Activity, and Optional Adoption for Other Banking Organizations — FDIC capital-rule changes can modestly affect banks' lending and financial stability, slightly shifting odds of a 2026 NBER recession but not directly determining NBER dating.
- 2026-03-19 [background] Regulatory Capital Rule: Regulatory Capital and Standardized Approach for Risk-weighted Assets — FDIC capital-rule changes could slightly affect bank lending and growth, but this is an indirect, uncertain influence on an NBER 2026 recession declaration.
- 2026-03-19 [background] Regulatory Capital: Category I and II Banking Organizations, Banking Organizations With Significant Trading Activity, and Optional Adoption for Other Banking Organizations — OCC capital-rule changes affect bank resilience and credit supply slightly, so they marginally shift recession odds but do not directly determine an NBER 2026 peak.
- 2026-03-18 [background] Treasury International Capital Data for January — Monthly TIC capital-flow data gives indirect context on financial conditions and may slightly adjust odds of an NBER 2026 recession.
- 2026-02-05 [A] Michelle W Bowman: Outlook for the economy and monetary policy — Bowman's Feb 5 Fed speech on the economic outlook can modestly shift expectations of a 2026 recession, affecting probability of an NBER 2026 peak.
- 2026-01-14 [background] Treasury, IRS issue guidance on the additional first year depreciation deduction amended as part of the One, Big, Beautiful Bill — Treasury/IRS guidance on enhanced first‑year depreciation can modestly boost investment and output, slightly lowering the odds of an NBER 2026 recession.
- 2026-01-09 [background] Consolidated Reports of Condition and Income for Fourth Quarter 2025 — Routine FDIC Q4‑2025 call reports give banking‑sector health data that slightly informs recession risk but cannot determine an NBER 2026 peak.
- 2025-12-01 [background] Regulatory Capital Rule: Modifications to the Enhanced Supplementary Leverage Ratio Standards for U.S. Global Systemically Important Bank Holding Companies and Their Subsidiary Depository Institutions; Total Loss-Absorbing Capacity and Long-Term Debt Requirements for U.S. Global Systemically Important Bank Holding Companies 12 CFR Part 324 RIN 3064–AG11 Final rule Effective Date: January 1, 2026 — New capital and TLAC rules for large banks effective Jan 1, 2026 slightly reduce systemic risk/credit shocks, marginally lowering odds of an NBER 2026 recession; effect is indirect.
- 2025-11-25 [background] Final Rule to Modify the Enhanced Supplementary Leverage Ratio — FDIC's eSLR rule alters bank capital/lending incentives and could slightly affect credit conditions and 2026 recession risk, but linkage is indirect.
- 2025-11-25 [background] Agencies Issue Final Rule to Modify Certain Regulatory Capital Standards — A final rule changing bank capital standards can modestly affect credit conditions and recession risk, but it does not directly bear on an NBER 2026 peak announcement.
- 2025-10-07 [background] Proposal Regarding Unsafe or Unsound Practices, Matters Requiring Attention — FDIC supervisory proposal signals bank-sector stress/regulatory concern that could modestly raise recession odds but doesn't directly affect NBER dating.
- 2025-07-10 [background] Regulatory Capital Rule: Modifications to the Enhanced Supplementary Leverage Ratio Standards for U.S. Global Systemically Important Bank Holding Companies and Their Subsidiary Depository Institutions; Total Loss-Absorbing Capacity and Long-Term Debt Requirements for U.S. Global Systemically Important Bank Holding Companies 12 CFR Part 324 RIN 3064–AG11 Notice of proposed rulemaking — GSIB capital-rule proposal affects large-bank resilience and thus macro risk slightly—an indirect, small influence on the chance of a 2026 NBER recession.
- 2025-04-18 [background] FDIC Announces Three Orders Against Discover Bank, Greenwood, Delaware — FDIC enforcement against Discover signals bank-specific stress that slightly raises recession probability but doesn’t directly indicate an NBER 2026 peak.

response.text: Regulators tighten capital standards effective 2026 and revise community‑bank leverage, while Fed/Treasury communications and bank call reports shift expectations—collectively nudging 2026 recession odds only marginally via credit and financial‑conditions channels. NBER dating remains pending, with markets watching BEA/BLS data, TIC flows, and bank‑stress metrics through 2026.
